channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Theater Tickets Are Cheaper in London Than New York. What Gives?
item.description: For half the price of a great seat at a Broadway show, you can see “Paddington” in the West End (if you can find a ticket) and snack on a marmalade sandwich.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson and Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: ‘Tristan und Isolde’ at the Met Opera Has a Troubling Problem
item.description: Audiences are packing the theater for a new “Tristan und Isolde.” Everyone can see the same spectacle, but they probably don’t hear the same sound.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Lise Davidsen as Isolde on Es Devlin’s set for “Tristan und Isolde.” From the Balcony, the performers, enveloped and hooded by the tunnels, sound dampened.

item.title: Don’t Let the Name Harry Hole Fool You. The Important One Is Jo Nesbo.
item.description: After a disappointing movie adaptation, the Norwegian author took the reins as showrunner in a new Netflix series based on his Detective Hole books.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:credit: David B. Torch for The New York Times
item.media:description: The actor Tobias Santelmann, left, stars at the title character in the writer Jo Nesbo’s “Detective Hole,” a new series-length Netflix adaptation, which Nesbo oversaw.

item.title: Outside Kennedy Center, Jane Fonda and Joan Baez Raise Voices in Protest
item.description: The pair joined in a gathering of artists and others who denounced censorship and faulted President Trump’s growing influence over the nation’s cultural life.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Alex Kent for The New York Times

item.title: New York City Ballet Pulls Out of Kennedy Center Performances
item.description: The decision by the company, one of the most prestigious in the country, is the latest in a wave of high-profile cancellations at the center.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney and Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Andrea Mohin/The New York Times
item.media:description: A scene from New York City Ballet’s “Solitude,” choreographed by Alexei Ratmansky, which was to be performed at the Kennedy Center.

item.title: ‘For All Mankind’ Is the Anti-‘Black Mirror’
item.description: The optimistic space-race drama, starting its fifth season, imagines humans settling space without tech colonizing our minds.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik

item.title: How ‘Love Story’ Gave Viewers a History Lesson on the Kennedys and ’90s NYC
item.description: The FX show, which dramatizes the relationship between John F. Kennedy Jr. and Carolyn Bessette, has inspired a legion of younger viewers to dig into the couple’s past and 1990s New York City.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.category: Love Story: John F. Kennedy Jr. & Carolyn Bessette (TV Program)

item.title: 5 Takeaways From Netflix’s ‘BTS: The Return’ Documentary
item.description: Netflix’s documentary about the superstar K-pop group’s comeback after a four-year hiatus showcases its creative process, brotherhood and anxieties about fame.
item.dc:creator: Regina Kim
item.media:credit: Netflix, via Associated Press
item.media:description: From left, the BTS members Jin, Suga, Jimin, V, Jung Kook and RM in a scene from the documentary “BTS: The Return.”

item.title: In ‘The AI Doc,’ Sam Altman and Dario Amodei Go on the Record
item.description: “The AI Doc: Or How I Became an Apocaloptimist” tries to cover so much that it ends up being more confusing than clarifying, but parts are fascinating.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson
item.category: The AI Doc: Or How I Became an Apocaloptimist (Movie)
item.media:credit: Focus Features, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A scene from “The AI Doc: Or How I Became an Apocaloptimist,” directed by Daniel Roher and Charlie Tyrell.

item.title: 33 Fun Things to Do With Your Kids in NYC This Spring
item.description: They can shake off those winter doldrums by hunting for Easter eggs, running the bases at Brooklyn Cyclones’ ballpark or gliding down Slide Hill on Governors Island.
item.dc:creator: Oliver Strand
item.category: Chamber Music Society of Lincoln Center
Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts
Museum of the City of New York
item.media:credit: Timothy A. Clary/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Participants in last year’s Easter Parade and Bonnet Festival on Fifth Avenue. It returns this year on April 5.

item.title: The BTS Connection With Howard University
item.description: In promoting its new album, the K-pop superstars looked back to a late-19th-century moment featuring the unofficial anthem Arirang for inspiration.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams

item.title: Can We Settle Your Music Debate? Of Course We Can.
item.description: Popcast is here to answer your questions. (And to tell you who’s right.)
item.dc:creator: Popcast
item.media:credit: Getty Images

item.title: Paul Klee’s “Angelus Novus” New York Debut On Hold Due to Mideast War
item.description: The rarely seen “Angelus Novus” by Paul Klee was supposed to arrive at New York’s Jewish Museum, but remains in Israel instead.
item.dc:creator: Marc Tracy
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Raphael and the Renaissance of Divine Beauty
item.description: This blockbuster exhibition at the Metropolitan Museum of Art humanizes a lapsed god of painting.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago and Marissa Alper
item.media:credit: Marissa Alper for The New York Times

item.title: Jon Bernthal and Ebon Moss-Bachrach on Bringing ‘Dog Day Afternoon’ to Broadway
item.description: Jon Bernthal and Ebon Moss-Bachrach are both making their Broadway debut in a high-stakes adaptation of the beloved 1975 film “Dog Day Afternoon.”
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:credit: Jonah Rosenberg for The New York Times

item.title: Overlooked No More: Gertrude Chandler Warner, Author of ‘The Boxcar Children’
item.description: Her best-selling series, about four children who live in a train car and solve mysteries, inspired sequels, spinoffs and animated films.
item.dc:creator: Miguel Salazar

item.title: Alexander Kluge, 94, Revolutionary Filmmaker in Postwar Germany, Dies
item.description: As a director, theorist and prolific author, he was one of his country’s towering artists and public intellectuals.
item.dc:creator: A.J. Goldmann

item.title: Stream These Movies and Shows Before They Leave Netflix in April
item.description: Several Christopher Guest mockumentaries and almost every James Bond movie are among the titles leaving for U.S. subscribers before the month ends.
item.dc:creator: Jason Bailey

item.title: Coleman Barks, Who Popularized the Islamic Poet Rumi in the West, Dies at 88
item.description: Although he did not speak a word of Persian, his interpretations of the 13th-century mystic’s work made Rumi a New Age icon for millions.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Vahid Salemi/Associated Press
item.media:description: Mr. Barks, center, in Tehran in 2006, receiving an honorary degree from the University of Tehran. The poet Robert Bly is second from right.

item.title: Five Horror Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month brings paranoid psychopaths, ungodly subjugations and fiery suspicions.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg

item.title: 9 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Late Night Disses Trump’s New ‘America First’ Award
item.description: “How many fake trophies that were made specifically for him is this guy going to get?” Seth Meyers asked on Thursday’s “Late Night.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Tracking Trump’s Efforts to Reshape Cultural Institutions
item.description: Jennifer Schuessler, a culture reporter who writes about intellectual life, is now covering President Trump’s attempts to amend the presentation of American history.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Rachel Wisniewski for The New York Times
item.media:description: A group of teachers posting signs on a former exhibit about slavery at the Independence National Historical Park in Philadelphia.

item.title: Salzburg Festival Fires Its Artistic Director
item.description: The board terminated Markus Hinterhäuser’s contract early, leaving the leadership of the world’s largest classical music festival in limbo for now.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone and Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Alex Halda/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Markus Hinterhäuser, who has been effectively dismissed as artistic director of the Salzburg Festival in Austria, in a 2021 photo.

item.title: ‘The Pitt’ Season 2, Episode 12 Recap: Breaking Points
item.description: The stalwarts of the Pitt seem to be cracking under the stress of an especially difficult day — in a workplace defined by difficult days.
item.dc:creator: Sean T. Collins
item.media:credit: Warrick Page/HBO Max
item.media:description: Dana (Katherine LaNasa) is losing her composure. She isn’t the only one.

item.title: Han Kang Among National Book Critics Circle Award Winners
item.description: This year’s winners include the latest novel by the South Korean Nobel laureate in literature and a memoir by one of India’s best known novelists.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:credit: From left: Mayank Austen Soof; Pascal Le Segretain/Getty Images; Maciek Jasik
item.media:description: Arundhati Roy, Han Kang and Kevin Young.

item.title: FKA twigs Says in Suit That Shia LaBeouf Had Her Sign ‘Illegal’ NDA
item.description: The suit said the nondisclosure agreement that was part of her court settlement with Mr. LaBeouf, her former boyfriend, effectively silenced her. His lawyer disputes that.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:credit: Andy Kropa (FKA Twigs) and Lewis Joy/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The filing is the latest chapter in a long legal battle between the singer FKA twigs, left, and her former boyfriend, the actor Shia LaBeouf.

item.title: Sam Kieth, Creator of Surreal Comic Book Series The Maxx, Dies at 63
item.description: A cocreator of Neil Gaiman’s Sandman series, he dreamed up The Maxx as a homeless character in the real world and a superhero in a subconscious realm. It was adapted for an MTV series.
item.dc:creator: George Gene Gustines

item.title: Robert Fox, Acclaimed Producer in Britain and on Broadway, Dies at 73
item.description: A favorite of actors like Maggie Smith, he produced dozens of plays, including “The Audience,” about Queen Elizabeth II, which was made into the Netflix show “The Crown.”
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:credit: Justin Leighton/for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mr. Fox in London in 1997. When he produced a show, “he was involved all the way, particularly in two crucial aspects: casting and editing,” the director Stephen Daldry said. “He had impeccable taste.”

item.title: ‘Yes’ Review: From Israel, an Uncomfortable Existential Howl
item.description: Nadav Lapid’s scathing tone and accelerated rhythms lead this movie about a married couple who ingratiate themselves with the country’s power elite.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: Dash Crofts, of the Soft-Rock Duo That Sang ‘Summer Breeze,’ Dies at 87
item.description: Seals & Crofts had a number of Billboard Top 20 songs in the 1970s before their chart topper brought them global fame.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Tom Hill/WireImage, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Dash Crofts performing with Seals & Crofts in Atlanta in 1977. The duo had a string of hits in the ’70s, including “Diamond Girl,” “Get Closer” and “We May Never Pass This Way Again.”

item.title: ‘Public Charge’ Review: The World Is Messy. Being a Force for Good Is, Too.
item.description: A new play at the Public Theater written by Michael J. Chepiga and the former ambassador Julissa Reynoso is a diplomatic memoir of sorts, and a meditation on loving one’s country.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes

item.title: ‘Teeth ’n’ Smiles’: A Pop Diva and a Flamed-Out Rock Music Revolution
item.description: The singer Self Esteem, a.k.a. Rebecca Lucy Taylor, is an incarnation of late 1960s counterculture in a new London production of David Hare’s “Teeth ‘n’ Smiles.”
item.dc:creator: Houman Barekat

item.title: The Kennedy Center Is Giving Bill Maher the Mark Twain Prize
item.description: The annual award that recognizes merit in humor is going to the satirist and talk show host who has been critical of President Trump and political correctness.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario and Marc Tracy
item.category: Mark Twain Prize for American Humor
Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)
Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Hunter Abrams for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Thank you to the Mark Twain people: I just had the award explained to me, and apparently it’s like an Emmy, except I win,” Bill Maher said in a statement.

item.title: Rare Books Stolen From a Jesuit Archive in Rome Are Returned to Italy
item.description: The books, about astronomy and other topics, were written in Chinese by Jesuit missionaries who shared information on science as part of their effort to spread Christianity.
item.dc:creator: Tom Mashberg

item.title: What’s Your Star Sign? The Ancients Could Offer You Some Insight.
item.description: Long before horoscope apps, the foundations of today’s multibillion-dollar astrology industry were laid in Babylonia, Egypt and the classical world.
item.dc:creator: Thomas Rogers

item.title: A Storied Rockefeller Art Trove Goes on View at Asia Society
item.description: Our critic offers a guide to 70 years of great devotional sculptures in the Asia Society collection — including some that he once helped install.
item.dc:creator: Holland Cotter
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas

item.title: When a Not-So-Dark Knight and His Sidekick Saved a Wacky Gotham
item.description: Joel Schumacher apologized for “Batman & Robin,” his corny 1997 superhero movie, but thanks to its ice puns and bat nipples, it’s since become an accidental parody worth howling at.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam

item.title: D. Boon’s Death Shook Punk Rock. 40 Years Later, Minutemen Look Back.
item.description: The influential band’s singer and guitarist died at 27, but the bassist Mike Watt and the drummer George Hurley never lost his spirit.
item.dc:creator: Bob Mehr

item.title: ‘They Will Kill You’ Review: The Co-Op From Hell
item.description: Zazie Beetz fights her way through a high-rise of horrors in a splatter-fest that concentrates its creativity in its gore.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: ‘She Dances’ Review: Steve Zahn Is a Reluctant Chaperone
item.description: Zahn plays the father of Claire (his real-life daughter, Audrey), awkwardly accompanying her and a friend to a competition in Kentucky.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: Mearns and Melnick, Dance Stars Who Choose Each Other
item.description: In “Superbloom,” the latest work by the ballet dancer Sara Mearns and the postmodern choreographer Jodi Melnick, women are at the forefront.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas and Vincent Alban
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Sara Mearns, left, and Jodi Melnick, who said, “We’re not a one-off.”

item.title: ‘Our Hero, Balthazar’ Review: Lost Souls and Found Follies
item.description: Opposites attract in this provocative drama about mass violence and two disaffected man-boys.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy

item.title: ‘A Magnificent Life’ Review: An Era of Artistic Revolutions
item.description: Sylvain Chomet (“The Triplets of Belleville”), directs this animated biopic of the filmmaker Marcel Pagnol, which revels in the sights and sounds of Provence.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: ‘Forbidden Fruits’ Review: Queen Bees of the Food Court
item.description: Among a group of young women at the mall, power becomes toxic in the style of modern classics like “Mean Girls” and “The Craft,” but without the vibrancy.
item.dc:creator: Chris Azzopardi

item.title: ‘BTS: The Return’ Review: Back in the Recording Booth
item.description: This documentary follows the K-pop supergroup BTS reuniting after a four-year hiatus as they find their album’s new vision.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu
item.media:credit: Roland De Courson/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: BTS performing on March 21 in Gwanghwamun Square in Seoul, as part of their comeback tour.

item.title: ‘Fantasy Life’ Review: Digging Out of the Doldrums
item.description: Matthew Shear’s comedic feature appealingly explores the jitters of a stalled young paralegal who babysits for a frustrated actress.
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Rapold

item.title: ‘Alpha’ Review: Body Trouble
item.description: A reckless incident at a party throws a young girl’s life into turmoil in this extraordinary third feature from Julia Ducournau.
item.dc:creator: Jeannette Catsoulis

item.title: Marriage Might Not Happen. But ‘Something Very Bad’ Did.
item.description: Executive produced by Matt and Ross Duffer, a new series by Haley Z. Boston finds horror in an idea that already terrifies her generation: romantic commitment.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg and Sela Shiloni
item.category: Something Very Bad Is Going to Happen (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Sela Shiloni for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Where is my fear of commitment coming from?” Boston recalled asking herself as she developed the story. “I was like, Let me go super deep into it and, you know, broadcast it to the world.”

item.title: The Thailand Biennale Brings More to Phuket Than Mass Tourism
item.description: Contemporary artists from across Thailand and beyond honor the island’s rich history of applied arts.
item.dc:creator: David Belcher

item.title: 8 Thriller Books About Housewives Getting Revenge
item.description: The author Elizabeth Arnott recommends thrilling tales of domestic vengeance and feminine power.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Arnott
item.media:credit: Author photo by Dan Kennedy

item.title: For Baccarat, This Artist Created a Different Kind of Chandelier
item.description: Harry Nuriev has created a chandelier for Baccarat that swaps some of its famed crystal with everyday objects.
item.dc:creator: Nazanin Lankarani

item.title: Josh Johnson Ridicules the Army’s Opening to Older Recruits
item.description: “Look, according to People magazine — and apparently the U.S. military — 42 is the new 35,” Johnson said on Tuesday’s “Daily Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Chip Taylor, Writer of ‘Wild Thing’ and Other Rock Hits, Dies at 86
item.description: The brother of the actor Jon Voight, he wrote songs for Frank Sinatra, Janis Joplin, Anne Murray and, with “Angel of the Morning,” Juice Newton.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler
item.media:credit: Ruth Fremson/The New York Times
item.media:description: Chip Taylor in 2003. “I wasn’t sure about ‘Wild Thing’ at first,” Mr. Taylor recalled in an interview in 1999. “On the demo I told the sound engineer to turn off the lights in the studio, and I just sat on a stool and sang it like a blues guy would.”

item.title: Jury Awards Chance the Rapper $35 in Legal Fight With Former Manager
item.description: The jury ruled in favor of the rapper, whose former manager, Pat Corcoran, was seeking $3.8 million in unpaid royalties.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Rebecca Smeyne for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chance the Rapper in Brooklyn last year. A legal dispute with his former manager, Pat Corcoran, ended with the rapper being awarded $35 in damages. The pair never put their deal in writing.

item.title: The Good List: 8 Things to Add Delight to Your Day
item.description: March Madness madness, better small talk and the morality of sandwiches.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch
item.media:credit: Illustration by John Gall. Flowers: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times

item.title: Pat Steir, Painter of Luminous ‘Waterfalls,’ Dies at 87
item.description: Spilling paint onto canvas and letting it streak down as it pleased, she often said that her celebrated works painted themselves.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Russeth
item.media:credit: Ike Edeani for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pat Steir in her studio in 2018, sitting in front of examples of her “Waterfalls” series, paintings born of ingenuity and accident.

item.title: Stephen Colbert Is Writing a New ‘Lord of the Rings’ Movie
item.description: In an announcement video with the director Peter Jackson, the late-night host said he was developing a new film based on early chapters of the trilogy.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins
item.media:credit: Scott Kowalchyk/CBS via Getty Images
item.media:description: Stephen Colbert will focus on writing a new “Lord of the Rings” movie after his talk show ends in May.

item.title: A Free Home for San Francisco Artists, From Dave Eggers and Friends
item.description: The writer, and the artist JD Beltran, have come up with Art + Water, to host exhibitions, give 30 artists studio space, and offer community events.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:credit: Poppy Lynch for The New York Times
item.media:description: The view of the bay from Pier 29, which will house Art + Water, a teaching and community organization. The first show will be devoted to the Bay Area filmmaker Boots Riley, and curated by an Art + Water co-director, René de Guzman.

item.title: Tracy Kidder, Author of ‘The Soul of a New Machine,’ Dies at 80
item.description: A Pulitzer Prize-winning narrative journalist, he wrote deeply reported books that often focused on heroic goodness in people.
item.dc:creator: John Schwartz
item.category: Soul of a New Machine, The (Book)
Mountains Beyond Mountains: The Quest of Dr. Paul Farmer, a Man Who Would Cure the World (Book)
Rough Sleepers: Dr. Jim O'Connell's Urgent Mission to Bring Healing to Homeless People (Book)
item.media:credit: Fred R. Conrad/The New York Times
item.media:description: Tracy Kidder in 1999 in Northampton, Mass. He moved to rural Western Massachusetts after completing a creative writing master’s program at the University of Iowa.

item.title: ‘Dog Day Afternoon’ Playwright Briefly Kept Out of Broadway Rehearsals
item.description: A week before opening night, tensions spilled over offstage, with the show’s producing team temporarily prohibiting Stephen Adly Guirgis from entering the theater.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Ebon Moss-Bachrach, left, and Jon Bernthal are starring in “Dog Day Afternoon,” a stage adaptation of the 1975 film. The show is slated to open March 30 at the August Wilson Theater in Manhattan.
